one_line_takeaway: SAND combines self-supervised contrastive graph learning (with a novel global clustering loss) and SHAP-guided one-shot NAS to push average hardware-Trojan detection accuracy to 98.1% and cut unseen-benchmark accuracy degradation to -3.9% (7 retraining epochs) versus -16 to -18% for SVM/AdaTest/GATE-Net baselines, at the cost of higher training overhead.
key_finding: On the full benchmark suite (Table 1), average detection performance was: SVM 81.3% accuracy / 0.80 recall / 0.80 precision / 0.80 F1; AdaTest 90.6% / 0.91 / 0.88 / 0.90; GATE-Net 92.2% / 0.89 / 0.92 / 0.90; SAND 98.1% / 0.98 / 0.98 / 0.98 -- an up-to-18.3% accuracy improvement over baselines as claimed in the abstract (e.g., on RISC-V, SAND reaches 90.4% vs SVM's 50.8%). On unseen-benchmark adaptivity (Table 2), SAND drops only from 98.1% (seen) to 94.2% (unseen), a 3.9-percentage-point drop with 7 retraining epochs, versus SVM (81.3% -> 65.2%, -16.1%, 38 epochs), AdaTest (90.6% -> 70.8%, -18.3%, 32 epochs), and GATE-Net (92.2% -> 75.4%, -16.8%, 22 epochs). An ablation removing the global clustering loss (Figure 5(d)) shows fragmented intra-class clusters and intermixing between benign/malicious embeddings, supporting the necessity of that loss term. Over 20 stability trials (Figure 8), SAND and GATE-Net show the most consistent accuracy, with SAND described as superior; SVM shows the largest fluctuation.
core_contribution: SAND is a hardware Trojan (HT) detection framework with two novel pieces the authors claim as their own: (1) an upstream self-supervised contrastive-learning encoder (3-layer GCN + readout + projection layer) trained on circuit graphs using a hybrid loss (positive contrastive loss, negative contrastive loss, and a novel global clustering loss that pulls circuits toward per-class centroids) to learn feature representations without manual feature engineering, and (2) a downstream classifier built via one-shot Neural Architecture Search (SuperNet training followed by SHAP-based pruning, using Shapley values to identify and prune low-importance SuperNet components) that adapts to new circuits/Trojan variants with minimal fine-tuning. Positive training pairs for contrastive learning are generated via three circuit-specific augmentations: logic-equivalent gate substitution (e.g., DeMorgan's law AND->NAND+NOT), subcircuit extraction, and relocation of independent sub-modules; negative pairs are Trojan-injected variants of the anchor circuit.
threat_model: Trigger-activated Hardware Trojans inserted by untrusted third-party IP vendors in the globalized semiconductor supply chain, evaluated as a binary (benign vs malicious) circuit/gate-level netlist classification problem. The paper does not model an adaptive/adversarial attacker against the detector itself; 'evasive Trojans' are treated as rare-condition trigger designs already present in the benchmark datasets, not as an adversary adapting to SAND.
limitations: The paper explicitly states SAND incurs higher training time, memory usage, and power consumption than baselines due to SuperNet exploration/maintenance (Figure 7), though exact numeric overhead values are not given in the paper (referenced only via a figure). No numeric ablation results are given for the individual contributions of the positive vs. negative contrastive loss terms independently (only the global clustering loss is ablated). The threat model does not consider an adversary who adapts Trojan design specifically to evade SAND's learned representations (i.e., no adaptive/white-box evasion evaluation against the detector itself). Trojan variety is limited to what the Cruz et al. insertion tool and Trust-Hub/synthetic sets provide; generalization beyond trigger-activated HTs to other HT classes is not evaluated. Only one run of averaged Table 1 results is reported (no confidence intervals/statistical significance testing for the 18.3% headline gain).

quantitative_results: Average accuracy: SVM 81.3%, AdaTest 90.6%, GATE-Net 92.2%, SAND 98.1%
Average F1: SVM 0.80, AdaTest 0.90, GATE-Net 0.90, SAND 0.98
Up to 18.3% detection accuracy improvement of SAND over state-of-the-art methods (headline claim)
Seen->Unseen accuracy drop: SVM 81.3%->65.2% (-16.1%, 38 retraining epochs); AdaTest 90.6%->70.8% (-18.3%, 32 epochs); GATE-Net 92.2%->75.4% (-16.8%, 22 epochs); SAND 98.1%->94.2% (-3.9%, 7 epochs)
RISC-V benchmark accuracy: SVM 50.8%, AdaTest 82.0%, GATE-Net 77.2%, SAND 90.4%
baselines_compared: AdaTest (Chen et al. 2023, reinforcement-learning-based adaptive method, ACM TECS)
GATE-Net (Wan et al. 2021, deep learning-based, described as state-of-the-art)
